text: The robotic surgical system is used to perform a resection of a mass located at the gastric cardia, with robotic arms manipulating instruments to excise the tissue.
The patient is a seventy nine year old male with a history of early lung cancer.
He was asymptomatic but was found to have an exophytic mass of the gastric cardia identified on a surveillance CT of the chest.
EGD revealed a submucosal mass and FNA returned suspicious for GIST.
A CT abdomen pelvis was then performed, showing a six centimeter exophytic submucosal mass at the gastric cardio along the lesser curve.
He was treated with preoperative Gleevec for five months, but this was discontinued when no change in size was seen.
This is his preoperative CT.
A large exophytic mass is visible near the gastroesophageal (GE) junction.
Due to the location of the mass, there was concern that he may need a total gastrectomy to avoid narrowing the G-junction.
Port placement can be seen in this diagram.
Three 8mm robotic trocars are positioned across the left upper abdomen, and a 12mm robotic trocar is placed in the right upper quadrant.
The surgeon opens the gastrohepatic ligament and performs circumferential dissection around the hiatus, revealing the anatomical structures in the upper abdomen.
A Penrose drain is placed around the distal esophagus to facilitate retraction and visually identify the location of the gastroesophageal junction.
The surgeon begins dissection at the inferior border of the gastric mass using monopolar scissors, with the GE junction visibly uninvolved in the procedure.
The surgeon continues circumferential dissection around the gastric mass, using robotic instruments to carefully separate the mass from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon adjusts the dissection margin, moving closer to the gastric mass to preserve more normal gastric tissue.
The surgeon rotates the gastric mass laterally and continues dissection on its medial aspect, revealing surrounding tissue structures.
The surgeon divides the final attachments of the gastric mass, carefully moving it aside to clear the surgical field.
The surgeon evaluates the gastrotomy to ensure it can be closed without tension and confirms that it does not involve the gastroesophageal (GE) junction.
The surgeon closes the gastrotomy in two layers, ensuring a secure closure of the gastric incision.
The surgeon begins closing the gastrotomy using an absorbable barbed suture in a canal fashion, ensuring the suture line is secure and tension-free.
The surgeon closes the second layer of the gastrotomy using braided permanent suture in a lumbered fashion, ensuring the closure is secure.
Following the closure of the gastrotomy, the surgeon conducts a leak test using an esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) to ensure the integrity of the closure.
The endoscope is inserted into the antrum of the stomach, providing a view of the gastric interior.
The endoscope is retroflexed to visually evaluate the integrity of the gastrotomy closure.
The closure of the gastrotomy is visible on the right side of the screen, showing the completed suturing layers.
The suture line shows no evidence of bleeding, and the scope passes smoothly through the GE junction, which does not appear narrow.
The surgeon desufflates the stomach and removes the scope after confirming satisfactory closure of the gastrotomy.
The specimen is removed through the twelve millimeter port site, which is then closed using an ovicral suture.
The patient was started on a clear liquid diet on post-operative day one and discharged home on post-operative day two on a regular diet.
The final pathology revealed a gastrogliomyoma rather than a just tumor.
